criterion_breakdown.label: Determinism and replay fidelity
criterion_breakdown.note: Frozen basis law, deterministic golden packs, replay attestations, and restore evidence all fit one doctrine cleanly.

criterion_breakdown.label: Coverage across engine, API, browser, native, and authority edges
criterion_breakdown.note: Every mandated family has an explicit home, so browser, native, and authority edges cannot silently disappear.

criterion_breakdown.label: Candidate-bound evidence quality
criterion_breakdown.note: The strategy is built around candidate hashes, compatibility hashes, and first-class gate artifacts rather than dashboard snapshots.

criterion_breakdown.label: Flake resistance and quarantine manageability
criterion_breakdown.note: Fast deterministic suites catch most drift before slower harnesses run, and blocking green posture remains unquarantined by design.

criterion_breakdown.label: Cost and runtime of the suite portfolio
criterion_breakdown.note: It is not the cheapest portfolio, but layered sequencing contains cost better than making every question an end-to-end run.

criterion_breakdown.label: Local developer ergonomics
criterion_breakdown.note: Most changes can be validated in seed-backed deterministic packs before reaching sandboxes, browser, or native devices.

criterion_breakdown.label: Failure diagnosability
criterion_breakdown.note: Each family emits typed artifacts, making candidate drift, admissibility failures, and product regressions easier to distinguish.

criterion_breakdown.label: Coverage of recovery, restore, and migration risks
criterion_breakdown.note: Replay, restore, migration, queue rebuild, and fail-forward concerns are first-class, not incidental afterthoughts.

criterion_breakdown.label: Accessibility and shell-continuity coverage
criterion_breakdown.note: Browser and native UI coverage is explicit and anchored to semantic, continuity, focus, and reduced-motion contracts.

criterion_breakdown.label: Ability to scale with later roadmap tasks
criterion_breakdown.note: Later QA, platform, migration, and release tasks can extend named families without redefining the test vocabulary.

alternatives.label: Layered, contract-first, candidate-bound portfolio
risks: Largest initial implementation surface because multiple harness families must exist together.
Requires disciplined fixture maintenance to keep golden packs and replay packs trustworthy.
strengths: Matches the corpus's explicit test-family law and release admissibility boundary.
Keeps fast deterministic suites below slower UI and resilience suites.
Produces machine-readable evidence that promotion and replay can reuse later.
alternatives.summary: Use validators and deterministic fixture packs as the foundation, then add model-based, API, browser, native, authority-sandbox, replay or restore, security, and canary layers, all bound to release-candidate evidence artifacts.

criterion_breakdown.label: Determinism and replay fidelity
criterion_breakdown.note: This option is strong on deterministic cores and property exploration for formulas, hashes, and state transitions.

criterion_breakdown.label: Coverage across engine, API, browser, native, and authority edges
criterion_breakdown.note: It under-serves browser, native, authority, and restore obligations that the corpus marks as mandatory.

criterion_breakdown.label: Candidate-bound evidence quality
criterion_breakdown.note: Unit evidence alone cannot satisfy promotion needs for authority breadth, client compatibility, or restore drills.

criterion_breakdown.label: Flake resistance and quarantine manageability
criterion_breakdown.note: Fast deterministic suites are reliable, but the option dodges rather than solves flake pressure in later acceptance layers.

criterion_breakdown.label: Cost and runtime of the suite portfolio
criterion_breakdown.note: The cheapest and fastest option because it intentionally keeps expensive acceptance and resilience layers thin.

criterion_breakdown.label: Local developer ergonomics
criterion_breakdown.note: Developers get the fastest local loop of the three alternatives.

criterion_breakdown.label: Failure diagnosability
criterion_breakdown.note: Core failures are well isolated, though cross-surface and cross-edge regressions are less visible.

criterion_breakdown.label: Coverage of recovery, restore, and migration risks
criterion_breakdown.note: Restore, replay, no-blind-resend, and client-window drift are too system-shaped to leave lightly tested.

criterion_breakdown.label: Accessibility and shell-continuity coverage
criterion_breakdown.note: Accessibility, focus, continuity, and native restore guarantees cannot be proven sufficiently with mostly unit or property suites.

criterion_breakdown.label: Ability to scale with later roadmap tasks
criterion_breakdown.note: The core harnesses scale, but later product and platform tracks would need to bolt on missing acceptance doctrine anyway.

alternatives.label: Narrow unit-heavy or property-heavy strategy with limited acceptance coverage
risks: Cannot prove browser, native, authority, or restore posture to the standard the corpus requires.
Leaves candidate-bound promotion evidence too dependent on inferred coverage rather than explicit acceptance artifacts.
strengths: Fastest local loop and strongest raw determinism on compute-heavy paths.
Lower infrastructure burden because fewer sandboxes and client harnesses are required.
alternatives.summary: Invest heavily in deterministic unit, formula, and model-based suites, while keeping API, browser, native, authority, and restore coverage intentionally light.

criterion_breakdown.label: Determinism and replay fidelity
criterion_breakdown.note: Wide system tests are the least stable place to assert byte-identical basis hashes or replay classifications.

criterion_breakdown.label: Coverage across engine, API, browser, native, and authority edges
criterion_breakdown.note: Broad flows can touch many edges, but they still blur where browser, native, or authority obligations actually live.

criterion_breakdown.label: Candidate-bound evidence quality
criterion_breakdown.note: A green scenario run is weaker promotion evidence than explicit suite-family artifacts with canonical hashes and scope.

criterion_breakdown.label: Flake resistance and quarantine manageability
criterion_breakdown.note: This is the noisiest option and would tempt quarantine-heavy release behavior the source law forbids.

criterion_breakdown.label: Cost and runtime of the suite portfolio
criterion_breakdown.note: Making wide sandboxes the main safety net is expensive and slow.

criterion_breakdown.label: Local developer ergonomics
criterion_breakdown.note: Routine development becomes dependent on running large environments or waiting on shared sandboxes.

criterion_breakdown.label: Failure diagnosability
criterion_breakdown.note: When a broad journey fails, isolating product drift from environment noise or stale fixtures is harder.

criterion_breakdown.label: Coverage of recovery, restore, and migration risks
criterion_breakdown.note: Some resilience cases can be exercised, but exact replay, reader-window, and restore evidence remain awkward to prove.

criterion_breakdown.label: Accessibility and shell-continuity coverage
criterion_breakdown.note: UI journeys can cover continuity, but without dedicated semantic packs they tend to under-specify accessibility invariants.

criterion_breakdown.label: Ability to scale with later roadmap tasks
criterion_breakdown.note: The portfolio scales poorly because each new capability tends to add more wide scenarios instead of reusable family doctrine.

alternatives.label: End-to-end-heavy browser or system-level strategy
risks: High flake pressure, slow feedback, and weak replayability when a run spans many mutable services.
Poor fit for exact candidate-bound evidence because root-cause isolation is weaker and reruns are noisier.
strengths: Feels intuitive because it exercises wide slices of the system at once.
Can expose some integration mismatches that narrower unit tests would miss.
alternatives.summary: Lean primarily on browser, native, or full-system sandboxes, using broad scenario tests as the main confidence signal and keeping unit or contract suites comparatively thin.

criteria.label: Determinism and replay fidelity
criteria.rationale: The winning doctrine must preserve exact-decimal outputs, stable basis hashes, deterministic outcome hashes, and explicit replay verdicts instead of tolerating broad pass/fail ambiguity.

criteria.label: Coverage across engine, API, browser, native, and authority edges
criteria.rationale: Coverage must stay balanced so deterministic engine confidence does not hide browser, native, or authority regressions, and rich UI coverage does not hide core-state blind spots.

criteria.label: Candidate-bound evidence quality
criteria.rationale: Promotion evidence must bind to one canonical candidate tuple, one compatibility boundary, and the exact suite artifacts or coverage contracts that actually proved the release safe.

criteria.label: Flake resistance and quarantine manageability
criteria.rationale: The test strategy must keep blocking suites deterministic by default, permit only tightly-scoped reruns, and keep quarantine or waiver posture out of green promotion evidence.

criteria.label: Cost and runtime of the suite portfolio
criteria.rationale: The portfolio must be expensive in the right places only, with deterministic fast lanes below slower browser, native, and restore suites.

criteria.label: Local developer ergonomics
criteria.rationale: Fast deterministic fixtures, stable seeds, and predictable acceptance harnesses need to be runnable in development without forcing full end-to-end sandboxes for every change.

criteria.label: Failure diagnosability
criteria.rationale: When a suite fails, the evidence model must tell the team what drifted, where, which candidate was involved, and whether the failure was product, environment, or admissibility noise.

criteria.label: Coverage of recovery, restore, and migration risks
criteria.rationale: Testing cannot stop at green request flows; it must cover stale reclaim, restore privacy reconciliation, queue loss, broker loss, and compatibility-window safety during release and rollback decisions.

criteria.label: Accessibility and shell-continuity coverage
criteria.rationale: Browser and native acceptance are not complete unless keyboard paths, reduced motion, semantic anchors, same-object continuity, and focus return remain contractually stable.

criteria.label: Ability to scale with later roadmap tasks
criteria.rationale: The doctrine should give later release, migration, QA, surface, and platform tasks a stable vocabulary of suite families, artifacts, and boundaries rather than forcing each task to invent its own harness taxonomy.

decision.runner_up_label: Narrow unit-heavy or property-heavy strategy with limited acceptance coverage
